title: Fancy
content:
آ آ آ آ Ever let the Fancy roam,
آ آ آ آ Pleasure never is at home:
آ آ آ آ At a touch sweet Pleasure melteth,
آ آ آ آ Like to bubbles when rain pelteth;
آ آ آ آ Then let winged Fancy wander
آ آ آ آ Through the thought still spread beyond her:
آ آ آ آ Open wide the mindï؟½s cage-door,
آ آ آ آ Sheï؟½ll dart forth, and cloudward soar.
آ آ آ آ O sweet Fancy! let her loose;
آ آ آ آ Summerï؟½s joys are spoilt by use,
آ آ آ آ And the enjoying of the Spring
آ آ آ آ Fades as does its blossoming;
آ آ آ آ Autumnï؟½s red-lippï؟½d fruitage too,
آ آ آ آ Blushing through the mist and dew,
آ آ آ آ Cloys with tasting: What do then?
آ آ آ آ Sit thee by the ingle, when
آ آ آ آ The sear faggot blazes bright,
آ آ آ آ Spirit of a winterï؟½s night;
آ آ آ آ When the soundless earth is muffled,
آ آ آ آ And the caked snow is shuffled
آ آ آ آ From the ploughboyï؟½s heavy shoon;
آ آ آ آ When the Night doth meet the Noon
آ آ آ آ In a dark conspiracy
آ آ آ آ To banish Even from her sky.
آ آ آ آ Sit thee there, and send abroad,
آ آ آ آ With a mind self-overawï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Fancy, high-commissionï؟½d: send her!
آ آ آ آ She has vassals to attend her:
آ آ آ آ She will bring, in spite of frost,
آ آ آ آ Beauties that the earth hath lost;
آ آ آ آ She will bring thee, all together,
آ آ آ آ All delights of summer weather;
آ آ آ آ All the buds and bells of May,
آ آ آ آ From dewy sward or thorny spray;
آ آ آ آ All the heaped Autumnï؟½s wealth,
آ آ آ آ With a still, mysterious stealth:
آ آ آ آ She will mix these pleasures up
آ آ آ آ Like three fit wines in a cup,
آ آ آ آ And thou shalt quaff it: thou shalt hear
آ آ آ آ Distant harvest-carols clear;
آ آ آ آ Rustle of the reaped corn;
آ آ آ آ Sweet birds antheming the morn:
آ آ آ آ And, in the same moment, hark!
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis the early April lark,
آ آ آ آ Or the rooks, with busy caw,
آ آ آ آ Foraging for sticks and straw.
آ آ آ آ Thou shalt, at one glance, behold
آ آ آ آ The daisy and the marigold;
آ آ آ آ White-plumï؟½d lillies, and the first
آ آ آ آ Hedge-grown primrose that hath burst;
آ آ آ آ Shaded hyacinth, alway
آ آ آ آ Sapphire queen of the mid-May;
آ آ آ آ And every leaf, and every flower
آ آ آ آ Pearled with the self-same shower.
آ آ آ آ Thou shalt see the field-mouse peep
آ آ آ آ Meagre from its celled sleep;
آ آ آ آ And the snake all winter-thin
آ آ آ آ Cast on sunny bank its skin;
آ آ آ آ Freckled nest-eggs thou shalt see
آ آ آ آ Hatching in the hawthorn-tree,
آ آ آ آ When the hen-birdï؟½s wing doth rest
آ آ آ آ Quiet on her mossy nest;
آ آ آ آ Then the hurry and alarm
آ آ آ آ When the bee-hive casts its swarm;
آ آ آ آ Acorns ripe down-pattering,
آ آ آ آ While the autumn breezes sing.

آ آ آ آ Oh, sweet Fancy! let her loose;
آ آ آ آ Every thing is spoilt by use:
آ آ آ آ Whereï؟½s the cheek that doth not fade,
آ آ آ آ Too much gazï؟½d at? Whereï؟½s the maid
آ آ آ آ Whose lip mature is ever new?
آ آ آ آ Whereï؟½s the eye, however blue,
آ آ آ آ Doth not weary? Whereï؟½s the face
آ آ آ آ One would meet in every place?
آ آ آ آ Whereï؟½s the voice, however soft,
آ آ آ آ One would hear so very oft?
آ آ آ آ At a touch sweet Pleasure melteth
آ آ آ آ Like to bubbles when rain pelteth.
آ آ آ آ Let, then, winged Fancy find
آ آ آ آ Thee a mistress to thy mind:
آ آ آ آ Dulcet-eyï؟½d as Ceresï؟½ daughter,
آ آ آ آ Ere the God of Torment taught her
آ آ آ آ How to frown and how to chide;
آ آ آ آ With a waist and with a side
آ آ آ آ White as Hebeï؟½s, when her zone
آ آ آ آ Slipt its golden clasp, and down
آ آ آ آ Fell her kirtle to her feet,
آ آ آ آ While she held the goblet sweet
آ آ آ آ And Jove grew languid. Break the mesh
آ آ آ آ Of the Fancyï؟½s silken leash;
آ آ آ آ Quickly break her prison-string
آ آ آ آ And such joys as these sheï؟½ll bring. 
آ آ آ آ Let the winged Fancy roam,
آ آ آ آ Pleasure never is at home.
meaning: About: John Keats (31 October 1795 ï؟½ 23 February 1821) was one of the principal poets of the English Romantic movement. During his short life, his work received constant critical attacks from the periodicals of the day, but his posthumous influence on poets such as Alfred Tennyson has been immense. Elaborate word choice and sensual imagery characterize Keats's poetry, including a series of odes that were his masterpieces and which remain among the most popular poems in English literature. Keats's letters, which expound on his aethestic theory of "negative capability", are among the most celebrated by any writer.
